name: West African Magic
description: West African Magic encompasses a diverse range of spiritual and religious practices deeply rooted in the beliefs and traditions of various ethnic groups across West Africa. It's not a monolithic system, but rather a tapestry of interconnected practices that emphasize the interconnectedness of the physical and spiritual realms. These practices often involve communication with ancestors, deities, and spirits through rituals, divination, and offerings. A central concept is the existence of a life force or energy that permeates all things, which can be manipulated for various purposes, including healing, protection, and prosperity. The origins of West African Magic are ancient, predating the arrival of Islam and Christianity, and are intertwined with the history and culture of the region. Methods include herbalism, divination using cowrie shells or kola nuts, invocation of spirits, creation of protective amulets (gris-gris), and elaborate ceremonies involving music, dance, and trance. Cultural significance lies in its role in maintaining social order, providing explanations for natural phenomena, and offering solutions to life's challenges. The impact of colonialism and the transatlantic slave trade led to the dissemination and adaptation of these practices in the Americas, influencing traditions like Vodou, Santería, and Candomblé. Today, West African Magic continues to be practiced and revered, serving as a vital link to ancestral heritage and spiritual power.
subtitle: Ancestral Power and Spiritual Connection

keyMyths.title: The Creation of the World
keyMyths.description: Olodumare, the Supreme Being, tasked Obatala with creating the earth. Obatala's actions and missteps shaped the landscape and the characteristics of humanity.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of Ifa Divination
keyMyths.description: Orunmila, the deity of wisdom and knowledge, developed the system of Ifa divination to provide guidance and insight into the future and the will of the gods.

sources.author: John Pemberton III
sources.work: Yoruba Religion: Spirituality in Africa and the Diaspora
sources.description: Comprehensive overview of Yoruba religious beliefs and practices.
